I caught up with him just now on the other room, but just in case I missed anything.

We have what people see on the outside, which is our services.
And then you have, like, what we're actually building to power that.
And our...
Customers today are the people delivering the service, not the actual people paying for the services.
And so what's cool about that is that, like, we actually have, like, multiple stakeholders that are heavily invested in giving us feedback and use it and empowering what we're doing, right?
But I think what's really interesting, was just actually Andy Chan, he was a general partner at Kotu.

He just came by as a friend, and he started his own fund now, and we're catching up, and we were just talking about this just now, and it was like, it's almost like cheating having services, because it's like, you don't spend, we're not spending a single minute building anything that we're not already getting paid for, which is insane, right?

Like, like, it's like, you're building, like, it's like a product market fit engine, you know, it's like people are paying you to deliver the outcome, and then you figure out how to do the outcome in the best way possible, you know?
Wait, so you just said, you just said, the customers today are the people delivering the service.
Are the customers tomorrow or the people delivering the service today?
Is that what you said?
No.
So essentially, our products' users, if you will, I should have said, are the ones delivering the service to clients, right?
And although our clients are our customers, they're not making a roadmap.
We're selling outcomes already, right?
Like, what dictates the roadmap is ultimately, like, the people using it.
But as we started to scale this thing, right, it was kind of interesting because we weren't going to fundraise at all.
And so we're probably, you know, generated like $500,000, $600,000 in cash, like, just in six months while me having a full-time job.
Then I quit, excuse me, and we started to build.
And then, like, as we started to scale this thing, we started to realize, like, the product here is not the service.
The product is, like, the workflow engine and learning from every time there's a workflow, like, learning from that, right?
then as we started
When we do that, we realize, like, hey, we need the best of the best of the best.
And these people want to know that at least someone has done due diligence on us, you know.
And so, like, we fundraised very favorable terms, but kept the valuation fairly low for exercise costs to be low.

And I'm sure Daniel showed you a bunch of this, but the part that's, like, really insane is that if you see, like, what the amount of productivity gains that developers and software engineers have had in the last 10 years, especially the ones that are using Cursor every day, they're using GitHub, they're using all of the tools, CI, CD, infrastructure as a code, like, all kinds of things, right?
Then you look at knowledge workers, and it's just, like, the delta is, like, night and day, right?

Like, and part of that is because imagine if, like, every single thought process you had of every single output you had in the last 10 years was actually documented as code, and all the activity was there, and your interactions were there, and your...
Versions were all there, and you had all those data readily available to put it into the context window of a model or to fine-tune a model.

Like, you would have an amazing, amazing thing, and that thing would be worth, I mean, I would have paid probably, I would have paid a year of the salary, like, to have that available for me, for my data, right?
But that doesn't exist because, like, like, knowledge work tools move away from code.
And so, like, really what we're doing is, like, getting back to the basics of why code works and then codifying knowledge work, right?
But the really cool thing with this is, like, our business model is just so efficient because of it because our average deal size is almost $160,000 a year.
We have a 48% win rate and average cycle of 29 days to close deals.
We have a pipeline.
We're booked through April.
We have 55 fields in our pipeline.
We've done zero marketing.
But part of this is kind of like a managing partner relationship, right, where the managing partners, if you're the managing directors, we call them, they're, like, the experts.
And they sell themselves.

So people want to work, they sell themselves, but they're part product development, part sales, part business development, part client facing, and then they have a small team under them that serves these accounts.
And then what we're building and what we're designing is that OS, if you will, that interacts with them.
Right.

And, but the really cool thing is like, it's kind like, we're getting to design what the business model of the NAI native company is going to look like in the future, which is like, to me is the thing that energizes me the most.
It's just like, how do we like, it's a new kind of company, you know, and there's no, we're writing whatever rules we want, you know, and, and that's the part that's really cool.
Cause we have so much product market fit and so much revenue growth and we're, we're profitable.
We're going to burn some money this year, but Q1, we're going to end profitably, you know, and they're like 70% plus margins, gross margins.
And so, so now it's like, we just need to think through, like, not just like, we need to really think through the experience, right?
Because there's a lot of different form factors this thing can take, right?
There's a form factor of, like, the design and the experience of if you're an editor delivering a piece of content for a client versus, like, do you want to build a cursor-like experience?
Do you want to build a lovable-like experience for knowledge workers?
Do we build, you know, micro apps?
Do we build an app store of micro apps, which are our workflows, right?
Like, what are the right abstraction layers of things?
It's, like, not an easy thing to solve, you know?

And, you know, and I think, like, it's, like, it has to be so tightly aligned between, like, what's possible, what is possible in AI, what's possible in engineering, and what's possible from a design and experience.
And I think everyone's just, like, after Chat2PT, everyone's just like, oh, it's just a chat, whatever.
It's just like, like, who said the chat window of typing is the best form factor in the planet, you know?
So, anyways, I'll shut up, but hopefully that gives you a little bit of extra...
This is fantastic.
I've done some sleuthing.
I checked out your posts on stats already.
It's just really impressive what you've been able to achieve in such a short time.
So you have about 25 customers, right?
Right now we're at 45.
There we go.
I'm already behind.
And what's the average lifetime that you anticipate for these folks on current offering versus what you imagine in the future?
Yeah.
So what we're seeing is we start by this combination of expertise plus hands-on grunt work, if you will, right?
Call it like the taskers.
And then the experts, they're like architecting and distilling how they go about doing the work.
And then the actual workflow is delivering the outcomes, right?
Right now we pick the lane of content itself.
But what we're seeing is like today I just had a prospect and they're like, hey, can you guys do case studies?
Like if we give you a list of our customers, can you reach out to them, set up an interview and do case studies for us?
It's like the answer is yes.
It's both like we're capacity constrained and we also need to pick our lanes, right?
A little bit.
But like as we scale and as the platform gets better, like that's a no-brainer.
Like we already build workflows to that all everyone we're recruiting, right?
Like so let me share my screen real quickly.

So for instance, like this one generates context on the company or this one is like people that apply, you know, and then it analyzes their video, analyzes their answers, you know, and then makes a final assessment on that candidate, you know, based on a bunch of those.
And then we have a recruiter that we hire that's the recruiter in the loop.
And then like, you know.
You know, kind of moving the process along and then setting that up, right?
So that combination, like when companies see this, they're like, oh my God, can I have this?
I will buy this as a service today and fire all my recruiting services and services I have, right?
And so the reason I bring this up is because we have customers like Galileo AI wants access to our platform to run all their sales processes as well.
They wanted to run all their product marketing processes of like understanding ICP and, you know, positioning, messaging, you know.
So it's like no one wants to buy a tool that's like a drag and drop workflow and then have to prompt everything themselves to maintain everything themselves and not learn from anything, right?
Whereas like what we're building is like the agent that builds that and learns from it.
But then the form factor, if, you know, it's really up to us, like right now, service is just attracting so much value.
It's like, it's insane.
Like, you know, people, our average deal size is like $13,000.
But we have people paying $40,000 a month, right?
And it's like including smaller companies.
And so it's like, why wouldn't we keep doing that, right?
And it's like a factor of like slightly less than a junior employee, you know, essentially.
But then especially in places where you don't need only one generalist, where you need a bunch of really good specialists.
Like marketing or content, you need a lot of specialists.
You need the head of content.
You need the SEO.
You need the editor.
You need a detector person reviewing things.

need a graphic designer, right?
Like you need a bunch of specialists, essentially.
In situations like that, the opportunity is kind of like ongoing, right?

I was more thinking of like, how do you imagine perhaps customers to evolve and or grow with your offer.
Yeah, that's a good question.
What we're seeing and what I'm hoping is that like, we start to kind of guide them along, right?
It's like, so all right now, all related to growth.
And so it starts with like, we create content.
Now you're getting traffic.
It's like, okay, how do you convert that traffic into distribution?
Okay, now you get distribution.

And in order to sustain that growth, need to hire people.

So there's that play.

And we already have an agent that builds these workflows to still require some engineering intervention.
Um, I need to commute back.
Um, no worries at all.
Okay.
I call you, I join back on, on my phone.
Does that be okay?
Sure thing.
Yeah.
Okay.
Take your time.
I've got time too.
Sounds good.

I'll join back.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss Renato Valdes Olmos' potential role as Head of Design at growthx.ai with Marcel Santilli.](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=94.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Renato has extensive experience scaling design teams (30+ at Honor, 80 at Lyft, 80 at Grammarly) and leading product/brand design, research, and product management.](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=268.0)
  - [growthx.ai operates a unique dual-business model: client-facing services and an internal AI-powered workflow engine, with \~45 customers and $160k avg. deal size.](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
  - [The company is exploring various product directions (editor tools, knowledge worker apps, micro-apps) to codify and enhance knowledge work productivity.](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1203.0)
  - [growthx.ai's business model is highly efficient (48% win rate, 29-day sales cycle) with strong financials (70%+ gross margins, profitable in Q1).](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1186.0)

## Topics

### Renato's Career Journey

  - [Started as a graphic designer, transitioned to UI/UX](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=120.0)
  - [Founded multiple startups (CardCloud, Human) with exits](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=160.0)
  - [Joined Honor as 5th leadership member, scaled design team to 30](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=240.0)
  - [Led 80-person design org at Lyft, redesigned core app](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=329.0)
  - [Grew Grammarly's design team from 5 to 80 in 2 years](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=380.0)
  - [Recent roles: EIR at VC fund, brief stint at Pitch (laid off)](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=644.0)

### growthx.ai's Unique Business Model

  - [Dual focus: Client-facing services + internal AI-powered workflow engine](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
  - [Product users are service deliverers, not end clients](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
  - ["Product market fit engine": Paid to deliver outcomes, then optimize delivery](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=943.0)
  - [Codifying knowledge work into reusable, learnable workflows](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1103.0)
  - [High efficiency: $160k avg. deal size, 48% win rate, 29-day sales cycle](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1120.0)
  - [45 customers, booked through April, zero marketing spend](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1126.0)

### Product Vision and Challenges

  - [Exploring various form factors: editor tools, Cursor-like experience, micro-apps](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1203.0)
  - [Balancing AI capabilities, engineering feasibility, and user experience](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1233.0)
  - [Potential to expand services (e.g., case studies, recruiting, sales processes)](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1394.0)
  - [Opportunity to define the business model for AI-native companies](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1159.0)

### Growth Strategy and Customer Evolution

  - [Guide customers through growth stages: content creation → traffic → distribution → sales opportunities](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1509.0)
  - [Expand services across marketing, sales, customer success, and hiring](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1530.0)
  - [Potential for API access to workflows](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1539.0)
  - [Developing a Cursor-like experience for knowledge workers](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1545.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Explore how Renato's experience aligns with growthx.ai's product vision and challenges](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=867.0)
  - [Discuss potential role in shaping the user experience and product strategy](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=867.0)
  - [Consider how Renato's team-scaling expertise can support growthx.ai's growth plans](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=867.0)
  - [Follow up on any unfinished topics due to Marcel's need to commute](https://fathom.video/share/yz2yPRGxrwxL1pWkxxUyNfyurMzGF9fi?tab=summary&timestamp=1622.0)
